Regulatory Apr 2, 2026

End Of An Era: Why The FAA Just Banned SFO’s Iconic Parallel Landings

No more wingtip-to-wingtip flying over the bay.

San Francisco's legendary parallel landings, a procedural marvel that maximized runway capacity during peak operations, are being eliminated due to evolving safety standards and modern aircraft performance capabilities.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 2, 2026

Why The Boeing 737 MAX Remains So Popular

Boeing's latest narrowbody aircraft continues to be a commercial success.

Despite past safety crises and lengthy grounding, the 737 MAX's enduring demand reflects airlines' confidence in Boeing's remediation efforts and their need for fuel-efficient capacity.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Apr 2, 2026

Réunion Island Lava Reaches the Sea

The February 2026 eruption at Piton de la Fournaise has lasted longer and produced a larger volume of lava than recent eruptions from this frequently active volcano.

Volcanic eruptions creating new coastlines can alter airfield drainage systems and ash dispersal patterns, requiring operational adjustments for nearby aviation facilities.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 2, 2026

Artemis II launches successfully on first crewed US Moon mission since Apollo

NASA’s Artemis II mission launched successfully on April 1, 2026, sending four astronauts into space on the first…...

Artemis II's successful launch validates NASA's deep-space architecture for sustained lunar operations, establishing the technical foundation for future commercial lunar logistics partnerships.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Liftoff! NASA Launches Astronauts on Historic Artemis Moon Mission

Spurred by American ingenuity, astronauts on NASA’s Artemis II mission are in flight, preparing for the first crewed lunar flyby in more than 50 years. NASA’s SLS (Space Launch System) rocket lifted off from Launch Pad 39B at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida at 6:35 p.m. EDT Wednesday,...

Artemis II's successful SLS launch restarts crewed lunar exploration architecture, validating heavy-lift capabilities essential for sustained deep-space missions and lunar base development.

See NASA →

Part 5's safety risk assessment framework provides operators a systematic methodology to identify and quantify hazards before incidents occur, shifting from reactive to proactive safety management.

Backtrack? Why American Airlines Is Worried Over Delta & United’s Passenger Experience Investments

The carrier is concerned by its key competitors in big ways.

American Airlines faces competitive pressure as Delta and United's elevated service investments risk eroding market share among premium leisure and business travelers.

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Blue Airbus A330: SAS Unveils Special Livery To Commemorate 80 Years Of Flying

SAS marks 80 years with a bold blue A330 and a symbolic New York debut.

SAS's blue A330 livery signals a strategic heritage marketing approach to reinvigorate brand perception among transatlantic travelers during competitive pressures.

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Artemis 2 launches on first human mission to the moon in more than 50 years

The first human mission beyond Earth orbit in more than 50 years is underway with an April 1 launch of four astronauts on a flight around the moon.

Artemis 2's crewed lunar flyby represents NASA's operational validation of deep-space human spaceflight systems before landing missions, establishing critical infrastructure for sustained lunar operations.

Air Cargo Apr 1, 2026

This Is How Much A Boeing 747 Freighter Costs In 2026

How has the aircraft's list price changed over the years?

Boeing 747F list prices have remained relatively stable despite inflation, reflecting limited competition in the large widebody freighter segment and sustained cargo demand.

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Organizations must document how they selected specific risk controls and justified acceptance of remaining residual risk to demonstrate compliance with regulatory requirements.

American Eagle Bombardier CRJ700 Diverts To Philadelphia Due To Nose Gear Steering Issues

Landing gear malfunctions remain a critical operational risk for regional carriers, potentially disrupting schedules and requiring immediate maintenance interventions.

Military Apr 1, 2026

New RAF Wing Commander aims to lead with integrity, empathy

Serving as the newly appointed Station Commander at Royal Air Force (RAF) Wittering, Wing Commander Joan Ochuodho is in charge of an operating base that employs 1,400 personnel and is populated by expert units fulfilling critical flight-training roles. Suffice it to say that in the three months...

RAF Wittering's new commander oversees 1,400 personnel at a critical flight-training hub, signaling institutional focus on leadership culture alongside operational excellence.

See Runway Girl Network →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Here’s When Delta Air Lines Plans To Debut The Largest Boeing 787 Dreamliner

Delta's Dreamliners will serve long-haul routes across the globe.

Delta's 787-10 deployment targets premium long-haul capacity expansion, signaling major carriers' shift toward larger wide-body aircraft to improve unit economics on international routes.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 1, 2026

Airbus Tests Armed ‘Bird of Prey’ Interceptor Drone

The ‘Bird of Prey’ platform was developed as a simple, cost-effective counter-drone system that can engage attack UAVs with low-cost air-to-air missiles. Airbus Defence and Space announced on Mar. 30, 2026, the first test of its ‘Bird of Prey’ counter-unmanned aerial systems (C-UAS) platform, based...

Airbus's armed interceptor drone addresses the escalating threat of hostile UAVs by offering a cost-effective alternative to traditional air defense systems for military and security operations.

See The Aviationist →

▶
General Aviation Apr 2, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Regional airports like CID drive pilot training pipelines by hosting flight schools alongside commercial operations, creating a talent development ecosystem for the broader aviation industry.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Military Apr 1, 2026

USAF E-3 AWACS Loss in Saudi Arabia Puts Spotlight on E-7 Wedgetail Procurement

The destruction of a U.S. Air Force E-3G Sentry AWACS in Saudi Arabia has reignited debate about the procurement of the E-7A Wedgetail. The loss of a U.S. Air Force E-3G Sentry AWACS (Airborne Warning and Control System) following the drone and missile attack at Prince Sultan Air Base, Saudi...

The E-3 loss accelerates the Air Force's transition to the E-7 Wedgetail, a more survivable platform with advanced threat detection capabilities against modern drone and missile threats.

See The Aviationist →

SpaceX files for IPO, opening Musk’s Moon and Mars push to investors

After months of speculation, Elon Musk’s SpaceX has filed for an initial public offering in the United States,…...

SpaceX's IPO move signals investor appetite for commercial space exploration beyond Earth orbit, potentially unlocking capital for competing lunar and Mars missions.

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

SpaceX quietly files for big bang IPO

SpaceX has taken a key step toward going public after confidentially filing for a potentially record-breaking initial public offering, according to multiple reports citing people familiar with the matter, in what space leaders hope is a watershed moment for the industry.

SpaceX's IPO filing signals institutional investor appetite for space infrastructure, potentially unlocking capital for commercial spaceflight expansion across the aerospace ecosystem.

See SpaceNews →

Military Apr 1, 2026

F-35 Crash in Nevada Leaves Pilot With Minor Injuries

Air Force says the aircraft went down on restricted federal property north of Las Vegas.

The incident's occurrence on restricted federal property suggests containment protocols worked as designed, limiting potential damage to populated areas and simplifying recovery operations.

KSQL Noise Abatement Departure

The San Francisco Bay area is complicated airspace, and right in the middle of it is San Carlos Airport. San Carlos airport is surrounded by terrain, San Francisco International class bravo,...

San Carlos Airport's noise procedures require pilots to navigate complex Bay Area airspace while managing terrain constraints and SFO's Class B airspace proximity simultaneously.

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Just 64 Miles: Delta Air Lines' 10 New Ultra-Short Flights Revealed

How many of them can you guess? And to think: Delta had even shorter flights in the past.

Delta's ultra-short regional routes suggest airlines are monetizing thin-margin point-to-point markets previously considered unviable, signaling a shift in network strategy.

Military Apr 1, 2026

At Top Speed, This Is How Fighter Jets Like The F-35 Perform

Fighter jets perform poorly at their theoretical max speeds which is why they almost never fly at those speeds.

Fighter jets operate well below maximum speed ratings due to fuel consumption, structural stress, and thermal limits, making supersonic dash capability more theoretical than operational.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Apr 1, 2026

Mesa Gateway Adds Light Aircraft Landing Fee

New landing fee announced just a week after those at Mesa's Falcon Field.

Sequential landing fee implementations across Mesa's airports suggest a coordinated revenue strategy that could trigger similar pricing across regional general-aviation facilities nationwide.

Picture of the Day: April 1, 2026

2020 Game Aero GB1 over Central Florida.

The Game Aero GB1's operational presence in Central Florida demonstrates emerging general aviation aircraft gaining regional traction beyond initial development phases.

Industry Apr 2, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport-airspace systems offers greater near-term environmental gains than waiting for new aircraft technologies alone.

See MDPI →

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Why Bigger Aircraft Are Sometimes Less Efficient

Do you know why this can be the case?

Larger aircraft's efficiency gains from economies of scale are often offset by increased structural weight, aerodynamic drag, and airport infrastructure constraints that reduce their per-seat fuel efficiency compared to optimally-sized regional jets.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Apr 1, 2026

Cirrus Launches New Simulator Training Program For SR Series Owners

Mission Ready Simulator Sessions is a free, scenario-based training program for SR20 and SR22 owners.

Free simulator training reduces owner liability and insurance costs while standardizing emergency procedures across Cirrus's installed fleet.

Business Aviation Apr 1, 2026

NetJets Expands Augusta Footprint As Masters Traffic Climbs

NetJets is building a dedicated private terminal at Augusta Regional Airport to streamline operations during the Masters.

NetJets' terminal investment signals sustained high-net-worth demand for major sporting events, creating a competitive infrastructure advantage over rivals.

Teledyne forms dedicated space unit to capture rising demand

The aerospace supplier is combining imaging, electronics and component lines under Teledyne Space...

Teledyne's consolidation of fragmented space capabilities into a single business unit signals industry confidence that space market growth justifies organizational restructuring to compete more effectively.

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

NASA’s X-59 returns to flight after false cockpit warning

NASA’s X-59 quiet supersonic demonstrator returned to flight after investigators determined that a cockpit warning light that cut…...

NASA's X-59 flight resumption validates confidence in the aircraft's systems after a non-critical anomaly, advancing the timeline for crucial low-boom supersonic data collection needed for regulatory approval.

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Artemis 2’s (nearly) 10-day flight around the moon

The Artemis 2 mission will send humans to the vicinity of the moon for the first time in more than 50 years on a mission lasting almost 10 days.

Artemis 2's extended lunar trajectory demands advanced life support and radiation shielding systems that will establish critical operational baselines for deep-space human missions beyond Earth orbit.

AI-powered drone analytics are enabling construction teams to optimize project oversight at scale, reducing costly delays through real-time site intelligence.

Military Apr 1, 2026

Saltzman: Space ‘baked into’ modern combat operations

Space Force personnel supporting operations under U.S. Central Command are positioned in the Middle East, at headquarters in Tampa, Florida, and at Shaw Air Force Base in South Carolina...

Space infrastructure has become operationally embedded in real-time military command and control, requiring distributed satellite support personnel across multiple geographic nodes rather than centralized bases.

Regulatory Apr 1, 2026

FCC eyes sweeping reforms to boost US drone power

The Federal Communications Commission is making its next big move in the race to dominate drone technology, and it’s tying that push directly to President Donald Trump’s national strategy for what officials are calling “American drone dominance.” more…...

FCC regulatory reforms for drone technology are now explicitly framed as critical infrastructure for U.S. competitive advantage, signaling coordinated government strategy beyond traditional aviation oversight.

Regulatory Apr 1, 2026

FAA Restricts SFO Landings

New FAA landing restrictions at San Francisco International Airport will cut arrival capacity by one-third.

SFO's 33% capacity reduction signals the FAA's prioritization of safety or airspace management over airport throughput, potentially rippling across West Coast route planning.
